chunks.text:
Experiences of online hate and abuse among women in politics
Experiences of online hate and abuse among women in politics
Online safety
Online abuse
Research, statistics and data
Published:
14 July 2025
Being online gives politicians space to share their messages, connect with constituents, and engage with political issues. But it can also expose them to online abuse and hate some of which is specifically targeted at women.  
Using a survey and individual depth interviews, Ofcom engaged with 23 women currently or formerly serving as MPs in the UK Parliament, to hear about the hate and abuse they have faced online. This qualitative research was conducted so that Ofcom, as the Online Safety regulator, could learn about these lived experiences directly from these individuals.
Content warning
This report includes content that may be upsetting or distressing.
Report
Read the full report.
The impact of online hate
This qualitative research examines the impact of exposure to online hate and hateful abuse on people with protected characteristics
Statement and Guidance: A safer life online for women and girls
Today we are publishing practical guidance for tech companies on creating a safer life online for women and girls.
Online hate and abuse in sport: a report by Ofcom in partnership with Kick it Out
A report examining the experiences of online hate and abuse faced by professionals working in sports and broadcasting.
